name: Yaman
aroha: Ni Re Ga Má Pa Dha Ni Sȧ
avaroha: Sȧ Ni Dha Pa Má Ga Re Sa
pakad: Ni Re Ga, Re Ga, Má Pa, Ga Re Sa
mood: Serene, devotional, expansive — often the first raga taught, and the threshold into an evening of music.
notes: Uses the sharp Ma (Ma tivra) of the Kalyan thaat. The full octave is available in both ascent and descent, which gives Yaman its open, unhurried character.

name: Bhairavi
samay: Morning, but sung to close any concert at any hour
aroha: Sa Re̱ Ga̱ Ma Pa Dha̱ Ni̱ Sȧ
avaroha: Sȧ Ni̱ Dha̱ Pa Ma Ga̱ Re̱ Sa
pakad: Ma Ga̱ Re̱ Sa, Ga̱ Ma Dha̱ Pa
mood: Tender, plaintive, devotional — the raga of resolution and farewell.
notes: All four komal (flat) swaras give Bhairavi its soft, pleading colour. By convention it is performed last, releasing the audience gently; in light-classical practice many ghazals and bhajans borrow its phrases.

name: Darbari Kanada
aroha: Sa Re Ga̱ Ma Pa Dha̱ Ni̱ Sȧ
avaroha: Sȧ Dha̱ Ni̱ Pa Ma Pa Ga̱ Ma Re Sa
pakad: Re Sa, Dha̱ Ni̱ Sa, Ma Pa, Ga̱ Ma Re Sa
mood: Grave, majestic, contemplative — attributed to Tansen, said to have been shaped for Akbar's court.
notes: Famous for the heavy, oscillating andolan on komal Ga. A deep, serious raga that unfolds slowly in the still hours after midnight.

name: Bhimpalasi
aroha: Ni̱ Sa Ga̱ Ma Pa Ni̱ Sȧ
avaroha: Sȧ Ni̱ Dha Pa Ma Ga̱ Re Sa
pakad: Ni̱ Sa Ma, Ma Ga̱ Re Sa
mood: Tender and yearning — a wistful, lovelorn afternoon raga that suits the ache of separation.
notes: An audava-sampurna raga: five notes ascending (skipping Re and Dha), all seven descending. Both Gandhar and Nishad are komal. Its pull toward longing makes it a natural fit for many ghazals.

name: Kafi
samay: Late evening; associated with spring and Holi
aroha: Sa Re Ga̱ Ma Pa Dha Ni̱ Sȧ
avaroha: Sȧ Ni̱ Dha Pa Ma Ga̱ Re Sa
pakad: Sa Sa Re Re Ga̱ Ga̱ Ma, Pa Dha Ni̱ Sȧ
mood: Light, romantic, folk-tinged — the raga of thumri, dadra, and the playful songs of spring.
notes: Komal Ga and komal Ni over an otherwise natural scale. A semi-classical favourite: relaxed and emotive rather than austere, which is why so much light-classical and ghazal singing lives here.

name: Patdeep
aroha: Ni̱ Sa Ga Ma Pa Ni Sȧ
avaroha: Sȧ Ni Dha Pa Ma Ga Re Sa
pakad: Ma Ga, Ma Pa Ga, Re Sa
mood: Warm and stirring — a bright afternoon raga with an undercurrent of pathos.
notes: Resembles Bhimpalasi but uses shuddha (natural) Nishad in ascent against komal Gandhar's softness, giving it a distinct, slightly brighter colour. A pentatonic ascent broadening to a full descent.
